---
title: "OpenTelemetry instrumentation"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{OpenTelemetry instrumentation}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Overview

'shinyOAuth' has built-in [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) instrumentation
via the ['otel'](https://otel.r-lib.org/) R package. When enabled, the package
emits **traces** (spans), **metrics**, and **logs** that give you visibility into 
the OAuth 2.0/OIDC flows of your Shiny app as performed by `oauth_module_server()`.

The OpenTelemetry instrumentation is completely opt-in. When the 'otel' package is 
not installed or no exporter is configured, all instrumentation calls are no-ops.

This is complementary to the R-level audit hooks described in
`vignette("audit-logging", package = "shinyOAuth")`. The audit hooks give you
R-native events you can route to any sink; OpenTelemetry gives you
vendor-neutral telemetry that easily integrates with various observability platforms.

## Setup

### 1. Install 'otel' and 'otelsdk'

```r
install.packages(c("otel", "otelsdk"))
```

### 2. Configure an exporter

OpenTelemetry is controlled via environment variables. At minimum you need to 
point the SDK at a collector or backend.

The easiest all-in-one option for local development is
[grafana/otel-lgtm](https://github.com/grafana/docker-otel-lgtm) — a single
Docker container bundling Grafana (UI), Tempo (traces), Loki (logs),
and Mimir (metrics), all pre-wired to accept OTLP on port 4318:

```bash
docker run --rm --name lgtm \
  -p 3000:3000 \
  -p 4317:4317 \
  -p 4318:4318 \
  grafana/otel-lgtm
```

Then set the environment variables before starting your R session:

```
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4318
OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-shiny-app
OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER=otlp
OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER=otlp
OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER=otlp
```

Open <http://localhost:3000> (admin / admin) to explore traces, logs,
and metrics.

> **Note:** [Jaeger](https://www.jaegertracing.io/) is another popular option
> but only supports **traces** — it will reject log and metric exports with
> HTTP 404. If you only need traces, Jaeger works fine; for all three signals
> use `grafana/otel-lgtm` or a full
> [OpenTelemetry Collector](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/collector/) setup.

See the ['otel' getting started guide](https://otel.r-lib.org/articles/otel.html) and
[OpenTelemetry environment variable documentation](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/otel/configuration/sdk-environment-variables/)
for the full list of configuration options.

### Preventing export from blocking the app

The `otelsdk` C++ exporter sends telemetry synchronously. If the collector is
slow or unreachable, the export call can block R's single-threaded event loop
and freeze your Shiny app. To limit the impact, set a short export timeout:

```
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TIMEOUT=2000
```

This caps each OTLP export to 2 seconds (default is 10 000 ms). Combined with
the batch exporter's schedule delay, this keeps worst-case blocking manageable.

shinyOAuth wraps every otel call in `tryCatch` so that SDK errors never 
propagate to the application, but the timeout is the only way to limit how
long a blocking export can stall the event loop.

### 3. Run your app

Now when you run your Shiny app with `oauth_module_server()`, 
it will emit traces, metrics, and logs to your observability platform.

## Traces (spans)

The package creates spans at key points in the OAuth 2.0/OIDC lifecycle.
The span hierarchy corresponds to the actual execution flow, including
across async worker boundaries.

### Span hierarchy

A typical OAuth login involves **two separate Shiny sessions** — and therefore
two independent traces. The first session redirects the browser to the provider;
it ends when the browser navigates away. The second session starts when the
browser returns with the authorization code and processes the callback:

**Session 1 (pre-auth redirect):**

```
shiny_session (SERVER, root)                        [main thread]
├── prepare_call (INTERNAL)                         [main thread]
├── redirect_issued (event)                         [main thread]
└── session_ended (event)                           [main thread]
```

The module detects no callback parameters and initiates the redirect.
`prepare_call` covers state generation, PKCE/nonce creation, state-payload
encryption, and authorization URL construction. The `redirect_issued` event
marks the moment the browser is sent to the provider.

**Session 2 (callback + authenticated session):**

```
shiny_session (SERVER, root)                        [main thread]
├── handle_callback (INTERNAL, manual lifecycle)    [main thread]
│   └── worker:handle_callback (INTERNAL)           [async worker]
│       └── handle_callback.process (INTERNAL)      [async worker]
│           ├── state_payload_decrypt (INTERNAL)    [async worker]
│           ├── token_exchange (CLIENT)             [async worker]
│           └── verify_token_set (INTERNAL)         [async worker]
│               └── validate_id_token (INTERNAL)   [async worker]
├── login_success (event)                           [main thread]
├── token_refresh (CLIENT)                          [later, if refresh occurs]
├── logout (event)                                  [main thread, if logout()]
├── session_cleared (event)                         [main thread, if token expires]
└── session_ended (event)                           [main thread]
```

The browser lands with `?code=...&state=...`. The module validates state,
exchanges the code for tokens, and the session becomes authenticated.
The `login_success` event is recorded on the session span when authentication
completes. Any proactive token refreshes also appear as children of this
second session's root span. If the user calls `logout()`, a `logout` event is
recorded. If the token expires or the reauth window is exceeded, a
`session_cleared` event is recorded.

(In synchronous mode, `handle_callback` and its children all execute on the main
thread without the `worker:` wrapper span.)

### Span catalog

| Span name | Kind | Where | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `shiny_session` | SERVER | main thread | Root span covering the entire Shiny session. Started on module init, ended in `onSessionEnded()`. |
| `prepare_call` | INTERNAL | main thread | Builds the authorization URL: generates state/PKCE/nonce, encrypts state payload, writes to state store. Attributes: `shinyoauth.provider`, `shinyoauth.pkce`, `shinyoauth.nonce`. |
| `handle_callback` | INTERNAL | main thread | Wraps the async dispatch of the OAuth callback (manual lifecycle). Ended in the promise `then()`/`catch()`. |
| `worker:handle_callback` | INTERNAL | async worker | Child span inside the mirai/future worker, linked to the main-thread parent via W3C context propagation. |
| `handle_callback.process` | INTERNAL | worker or main | The actual callback processing logic (state validation, token exchange, verification). |
| `token_exchange` | CLIENT | worker or main | The HTTP POST to the token endpoint. Includes `http.request.method` and `url.full` attributes. |
| `verify_token_set` | INTERNAL | worker or main | Token response validation (type check, scope reconciliation, ID token verification). |
| `validate_id_token` | INTERNAL | worker or main | JWT signature verification and claims validation of the ID token. |
| `state_payload_decrypt` | INTERNAL | worker or main | AES-GCM decryption and validation of the OAuth state payload. |
| `userinfo_jwt_decode` | INTERNAL | worker or main | Decoding and verifying a JWT-encoded userinfo response. |
| `client_assertion_build` | INTERNAL | worker or main | Building a `client_secret_jwt` or `private_key_jwt` client assertion. |
| `token_refresh` | CLIENT | worker or main | Token refresh HTTP call (sync path). |
| `worker:token_refresh` | INTERNAL | async worker | Child span for async token refresh in a worker. |
| `get_userinfo` | CLIENT | worker or main | Fetches user info from the provider's userinfo endpoint. |
| `userinfo_jwt_decode` | INTERNAL | worker or main | Decoding and verifying a JWT-encoded userinfo response. |
| `oidc_discovery` | CLIENT | main thread | OIDC discovery document fetch and parsing. |
| `jwks_fetch` | CLIENT | worker or main | JWKS key set fetch from the provider's JWKS URI (cache-miss path only). |
| `validate_id_token` | INTERNAL | worker or main | JWT signature verification and claims validation of the ID token. |
| `state_payload_decrypt` | INTERNAL | worker or main | AES-GCM decryption and validation of the OAuth state payload. |
| `client_assertion_build` | INTERNAL | worker or main | Building a `client_secret_jwt` or `private_key_jwt` client assertion. |
| `revoke_token` | CLIENT | worker or main | Token revocation HTTP call. |
| `worker:revoke_token` | CLIENT | async worker | Child span for async token revocation. |
| `introspect_token` | CLIENT | worker or main | Token introspection HTTP call. |
| `worker:introspect_token` | CLIENT | async worker | Child span for async token introspection. |

### Span attributes

All spans include relevant context as attributes. The namespace is `shinyoauth.*`:

- `shinyoauth.session_id` — hashed Shiny session token (correlates all spans from the same browser session)
- `shinyoauth.provider` — provider name (e.g., `"github"`, `"google"`)
- `shinyoauth.issuer` — OIDC issuer URL
- `shinyoauth.client_id_digest` — hashed client ID (never the raw value)
- `shinyoauth.module_id` — Shiny module namespace ID
- `shinyoauth.async` — whether the operation ran in an async worker
- `shinyoauth.which` — for revocation/introspection: `"access"` or `"refresh"`
- `shinyoauth.is_refresh` — for token verification: whether this is a refresh flow
- `shinyoauth.url` — target URL for userinfo requests

Note: sensitive identifiers (session token, client ID) are always hashed with
HMAC-SHA256 via `string_digest()` before being set as span attributes.
This to avoid exposing raw sensitive values in telemetry while still allowing correlation and analysis based on those attributes.
Digest keys are randomly generated if not set; you can set a stable one via `options(shinyOAuth.audit_digest_key = ...)`.

## Metrics

The package records the following metrics when a metrics exporter is configured:

| Metric name | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `shinyoauth.login.total` | Counter | Login attempts. Attributes: `success` (logical), `provider`. |
| `shinyoauth.token_refresh.total` | Counter | Token refresh attempts. Attributes: `success` (logical), `provider`. |
| `shinyoauth.token_revocation.total` | Counter | Token revocation attempts. Attributes: `success` (logical), `provider`. |
| `shinyoauth.token_exchange.duration_seconds` | Histogram | Duration of token exchange HTTP calls. Attributes: `provider`. |
| `shinyoauth.token_refresh.duration_seconds` | Histogram | Duration of token refresh HTTP calls. Attributes: `provider`. |
| `shinyoauth.userinfo.duration_seconds` | Histogram | Duration of userinfo endpoint HTTP calls. Attributes: `provider`. |
| `shinyoauth.oidc_discovery.duration_seconds` | Histogram | Duration of OIDC discovery document fetch and parsing. Attributes: `issuer`. |
| `shinyoauth.active_sessions` | UpDownCounter | Number of currently active Shiny sessions (incremented on session start, decremented on session end). |

These metrics are useful for dashboards and alerting; for example, monitoring 
login success rates, detecting refresh failures, or tracking concurrent session counts.

## Logs

When a log exporter is configured, the package emits structured log records at 
two levels:

- `log_info` — for every audit event (e.g., `"audit:session_started"`,
  `"audit:login_success"`, `"audit:token_revocation"`). These mirror the events
  described in `vignette("audit-logging", package = "shinyOAuth")`, but routed
  through the OpenTelemetry log pipeline instead of (or in addition to) the R-level 
  audit hooks.

- `log_error` — for every error raised by the package (token errors, HTTP 
  failures, transport errors, state validation failures, etc.). Each log record 
  includes the error class and the package's internal trace ID for correlation.
